mnemonics.title: RST for Equivalence
mnemonics.content: RST: Reflexive-Symmetric-Transitive - remember as 'Really Simple Thing' for equivalence relation.

mnemonics.title: RAT for Partial Order
mnemonics.content: RAT: Reflexive-Antisymmetric-Transitive - remember as 'Really Awful Thing' for partial order.

mnemonics.title: In/Out Degree
mnemonics.content: In-degree: edges going INto vertex. Out-degree: edges going OUT of vertex.

mnemonics.title: DAG Meaning
mnemonics.content: DAG = Directed Acyclic Graph - think 'DAG' as 'Day' has no cycles (days don't repeat in the same instance).

acronyms.usage: Used for topological sorting and task scheduling with dependencies.
Three properties that define an equivalence relation.
Three properties that define a partial order relation.
Maximal subgraph where directed paths exist between all vertex pairs.

visualTips.visualization: Think of it as a 2D table where rows are 'from' vertices and columns are 'to' vertices. Fill 1 where arrows point.
Draw an arrow → to show direction. The arrow tail is start, arrowhead is end.
A→B→C means A can reach C through B. Draw it as a chain of dominoes falling.

visualTips.visualize: Imagine you can travel by one-way streets from any location to any other location.

analogies.analogy: Like a one-way street system in a city - every road has a specific direction you must follow.
Like a list of allowed pairings - e.g., which students can pair with which mentors.
Everyone is friends with themselves - like looking in a mirror.
Like being Facebook friends - if A is friends with B, B is friends with A.
